verbatim_text: So what does this tell us about scaling flex agent or running example? Well, this tells us that we need to train a smaller model on much more data. This also tells us that we need to scale up our reasoning and use more inference time samples, for instance. So my work on scaling has had some recognition in the media. Recently there was a VentureBeat article about our new T-Sword scaling law. Folks from various industry labs have been writing about our new scaling law in their presentations and so on. Jane Street, it turns out, wants to deploy something like this and actually publish academic research in machine learning for the first time to my knowledge in this area. And also some of my other scaling law work, which I'll tell you about in a moment, we found out later on that OpenAI was doing a very similar thing. And so that was also some industry validation. This is also part of a longer work on this area of scaling. I've also done work on constructing LLMs with different architectures that combine components from these different architectures. Like there are these new linear models or state space models that are proposed as alternatives to the famous transformer architecture. But it's really expensive to experiment with these different combinations and so one of the things that I designed is this algorithm that produces these pre-trained hybrids from existing pre-trained models. And this is scaling because it kind of bypasses this pre-training scaling decision altogether.
